title: In-Chat Identity Verification (Platform Passport)
description: Allows users to securely share verified identity data (name, DoB, address, ID document scans) with third-party services via the platform interface. Data is end-to-end encrypted between user and requesting service — the platform server never has plaintext access to identity documents. Architecturally equivalent to Telegram Passport but with stronger cryptographic guarantees.

cryptographic_design.model: Client-side encryption with service public key
flow: 1. User stores identity data locally, encrypted with a user-controlled symmetric key (AES-256-GCM, derived from passphrase via Argon2id).
2. Requesting service provides its RSA-4096 or X25519 public key in the authorization request.
3. Client decrypts local data, re-encrypts with service's public key (hybrid: ECDH-derived AES key + RSA/X25519 wrapper).
4. Encrypted payload transmitted through platform relay to service.
5. Platform relay sees only ciphertext — zero plaintext access.

key_derivation.salt: User-specific, stored server-side (non-secret, used to reproduce key on any device)

cryptographic_design.forward_secrecy: Each sharing session generates a fresh ephemeral key pair. Old sessions cannot be decrypted even if user's master key is later compromised.

server_role.stores: Encrypted blobs only. Schema: { user_id, data_type, encrypted_blob BYTEA, iv BYTEA, created_at, updated_at }
server_role.does_not_store: Any plaintext identity data, key material, or decryption capability
server_role.audit_log: service_id + user_id + data_types_shared + timestamp stored for user's own audit trail (not shared with service)

server_validates: JWT signature against registered service's signing key (pre-registered, not from JWT itself).
nonce not seen before (Redis SET NX with TTL=15min).
requested_fields are subset of supported types.
callback_url matches registered service redirect whitelist.

security.man_in_middle: Platform relay cannot intercept: ciphertext opaque. TLS in transit.
security.replay_attack: JWT nonce validated with Redis idempotency. Each authorization request is single-use.
security.malicious_service: Service cannot request more fields than user authorizes. User sees exact list of requested fields before sharing.
security.data_at_rest: Encrypted blobs at rest (Postgres column encrypted with pgcrypto + column-level key in key management service).
security.gdpr_right_to_erasure: DELETE /v1/passport/data → wipes all blobs immediately. Service does not get notified (platform has no plaintext to 'un-share').

ui.setup: Settings → Privacy → Platform Passport → Set up → Passphrase entry → Add identity fields one by one
ui.sharing_flow: Authorization request arrives → bottom sheet shows: service name, requested fields, 'Share' / 'Deny'. Passphrase re-entry for high-sensitivity fields.
ui.audit_log: Settings → Passport → 'Sharing history' — list of past authorizations with date and service name

compliance.gdpr: User is data controller for their own identity data. Platform is processor (encrypted storage only).
compliance.pci_dss: No payment card data in this flow. Payments handled separately (B-122).
